<!-- LTeX: language=en-US -->
# Review of Probability and Statistics {#sec-probstats}

::: {.hidden}
$$
\require{color}
\newcommand{\highlight}[2][yellow]{\mathchoice%
  {\colorbox{#1}{$\displaystyle#2$}}%
  {\colorbox{#1}{$\displaystyle#2$}}%
  {\colorbox{#1}{$\displaystyle#2$}}%
  }%
$$
:::

Regression analysis rests on a probabilistic framework: the data we observe are assumed to be realizations of an underlying random process. This chapter builds the vocabulary needed to make that idea precise. The central concept we work toward is the **data-generating process (DGP)**; i.e the probability model that we imagine produced the observed data. Understanding the DGP is the key to understanding what regression estimates, and what it cannot tell us.

## Where Does Data Come From? {#sec-where-data}

Suppose your class conducts a short test, admittedly a bit strange one: Each student measures the **length of their hair (in cm)** and performs a **jump and reach test** — jumping as high as possible and **recording the height reached (in cm)**. The result is a dataset with two columns and $n$ rows — one row per student.

![](images/reach-jump.gif){fig-align="center" width="40%"}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🤓 I generated the data artificially. That is, I know the true **usually unknown** data generating process from which the data is sampled. That's a didactic hack that allows me to demonstrate the difference between estimated quantities and their typically unknown true counterparts. 

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
# Code to Generate the Jump-Test Data
```{r}
## True simple linear regression data-generating process
set.seed(32123)
n     <- 30
beta1 <- 77     ## intercept
beta2 <- -1.75  ## slope (negative: more hair → lower jump & reach scores)
sigma <- 5      ## error standard deviation

## Generate the predictor X: hair length ~ 50/50 mixture of two normal components
mu_X_m     <-  7; sigma_X_m <- 3   ## short-hair component
mu_X_f     <- 27; sigma_X_f <- 7   ## long-hair component

gender     <- sample(c("female", "male"), size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.5, 0.5))

hairlength <- round(pmax(
  ifelse(gender == "female", rnorm(n, mu_X_f, sigma_X_f),
                             rnorm(n, mu_X_m, sigma_X_m)), 1), 2)

## Generate the outcome Y: Y_i = beta1 + beta2*X_i + eps_i, with eps_i ~ N(0, sigma^2)

jumpreach <- round(pmax(beta1 + beta2 * hairlength + rnorm(n, 0, sigma), 0), 2)

gender01  <- ifelse(gender == "female", 1, 0)

## Data frame (the only thing we observe usually)
jumptest_data <- data.frame(hairlength = hairlength,
                            jumpreach  = jumpreach, 
                            gender     = gender, 
                            gender01   = gender01)

write.csv(jumptest_data, file = "DATA/jumptest_data.csv", row.names = FALSE)

## Marginal distribution of Y (derived analytically by marginalising over X):
##   Y ~ 0.5*N(mu_Y_m, sigma_Y_m^2) + 0.5*N(mu_Y_f, sigma_Y_f^2)
mu_Y_m    <- beta1 + beta2 * mu_X_m
sigma_Y_m <- sqrt(beta2^2 * sigma_X_m^2 + sigma^2)
mu_Y_f    <- beta1 + beta2 * mu_X_f
sigma_Y_f <- sqrt(beta2^2 * sigma_X_f^2 + sigma^2)

## True distribution functions (usually unknown)
f_X   <- function(x) 0.5*dnorm(x, mu_X_m, sigma_X_m) + 0.5*dnorm(x, mu_X_f, sigma_X_f)
F_X   <- function(x) 0.5*pnorm(x, mu_X_m, sigma_X_m) + 0.5*pnorm(x, mu_X_f, sigma_X_f)
f_Y   <- function(y) 0.5*dnorm(y, mu_Y_m, sigma_Y_m) + 0.5*dnorm(y, mu_Y_f, sigma_Y_f)
F_Y   <- function(y) 0.5*pnorm(y, mu_Y_m, sigma_Y_m) + 0.5*pnorm(y, mu_Y_f, sigma_Y_f)
f_YgX <- function(y, x) dnorm(y, beta1 + beta2 * x, sigma)  ## conditional density


```
:::
:::


```{r}
#| label: tbl-class-data
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "First six rows of the jump and reach test data set."

library("kableExtra")
kbl(head(jumptest_data[,1:2]),
    col.names = c("Hair Length (cm)", "Jump Height (cm)"),
    align = c("r", "r")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```



Even from the first six rows, we see two things:

1. There appears to be a pattern: shorter hair tends to go together with higher jump and reach scores.
2. The relationship is noisy — knowing someone's hair length does not tell us exactly what jump and reach score they will achieve.

@fig-class-scatter displays all $n = 30$ observations. The downward trend is visible, but so is the variability around it.

```{r}
#| label: fig-class-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Hair length and jump and reach scores for 30 students. A clear negative association is visible, but the relationship is not deterministic."

plot(jumptest_data$hairlength, jumptest_data$jumpreach,
     xlab = "Hair Length (cm)",
     ylab = "Jump Height (cm)",
     pch  = 19,
     col  = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.7))
```

🤔 **Now ask yourself:** What would happen if a *different* group of 30 students had filled in the survey? 

🤓 You would get different hair lengths and different jump and reach scores for each student. The overall pattern might persist, but every individual number would change. The dataset is, in this sense, **random**: it is one particular outcome of a process that could have produced many different outcomes. Key-word: **sampling variability**

This observation is the starting point of statistics. The data we observe are a **realization** of a random process. Had the process run differently (different students, a different year), we would have seen different data. The **data-generating process (DGP)** is the underlying probability model that governs this random process. Everything in this chapter builds toward a useful formulation of the DGP.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Guiding Questions

1. What mathematical objects describe the randomness in a single measurement, such as the jump and reach score of a randomly chosen student?
2. How do we describe the *relationship* between two random measurements, such as hair length and jump and reach scores?
3. What is a data-generating process, and how does it connect to the regression model in the next chapter?
:::


## Random Variables and Distributions {#sec-rv}

The key mathematical concept is the **random variable**. You can view a random variable as a data generator.

A **random variable**, say $X,$ can gives us values (e.g. numeric values like $0.4, 1.9, \dots$ or qualitative values like `red`, `blue`, etc.) based on an underlying (typically unknown) law: **its distribution**. 

Before we observe the realized value, $X$ is uncertain — it could take many different values. After observing it, we have a **realization**: one specific value that $X$ happened to take. For this realized, no-longer-uncertain value we write 
$$
X_{\operatorname{obs}}.
$$
The subscript **"obs"** is a reminder that the value has been **obs**erved and is now a fixed number, not a random quantity. We reserve the upper-case letter $X$ without subscript for the random variable itself — uncertain, not yet pinned down to a single value.



#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}


* **Random Variable $Y$:** The jump and reach score of a randomly drawn student is a random variable, which we call $Y$. This random variable can take any non-negative value $\geq 0.$
* **Random Variable $X$:** The hair length of a randomly drawn student is another random variable, $X$. This random variable can take any non-negative value $\geq 0.$

Our class has $n$ students, indexed by $i = 1, \ldots, n$. The hair length and jump and reach score of student $i$ are the **random variable pairs** 
$$
(Y_i, X_i),
\qquad i = 1, \ldots, n.
$$ 
Once student $i$ fills in the survey, we record the **observed value pair**
$$
(Y_{i,\operatorname{obs}}, X_{i,\operatorname{obs}}),
\qquad i = 1, \ldots, n.
$$

The full dataset consists of $n$ pairs (one pair per student): 
$$
(Y_{1,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{1,\operatorname{obs}}),\, (Y_{2,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{2,\operatorname{obs}}),\, \ldots,\, (Y_{n,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{n,\operatorname{obs}})
$$ 
In our jump-test data set (@tbl-class-data) we have $n = 30$ students.


@fig-hairlength-anim shows possible realizations of the random variable, I used to generate the hair length data in our jump test example. Each observed realization $(Y_{i,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{i,\operatorname{obs}})$ corresponds to a possible realization of the underlying random variable pair $(Y_i,\, X_i,),$ of student $i=1,\dots,n$. The dashed black curves are the true (usually unknown) densities $f_X(x)$ and $f_Y(x)$: realizations tend to cluster where the densities are large.

```{r}
#| label: fig-hairlength-anim
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Sequential realizations of the paired random variables $(X_i, Y_i)$. **Top:** hair-length realizations drawn from the (usually unknown) mixture density $f_X(x)$ (dashed black). **Bottom:** the corresponding jump-and-reach realizations drawn from the (usually unknown) marginal density $f_Y(y)$ (dashed black). Each frame adds one student; the two panels are always in step."
#| fig.show: animate
#| animation.hook: gifski
#| interval: 0.4
#| fig-height: 6

set.seed(42)
n_anim  <- 30
g_anim  <- rbinom(n_anim, 1, 0.5)
x_anim  <- round(pmax(
  ifelse(g_anim == 1, rnorm(n_anim, mu_X_f, sigma_X_f),
                      rnorm(n_anim, mu_X_m, sigma_X_m)), 1), 2)
y_anim  <- round(pmax(beta1 + beta2 * x_anim + rnorm(n_anim, 0, sigma), 0), 2)

for (k in seq_len(n_anim)) {
  par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

  ## Top panel: X realizations under f_X
  curve(f_X(x),
        from = -5, to = 55,
        ylim = c(-0.008, 0.075),
        xlab = "Hair Length (cm)",
        ylab = expression(f[X](x)),
        lwd  = 2, lty  = 2, col  = "black",
        main = paste0("Student ", k, " of ", n_anim),
        bty  = "n")
  abline(h = 0, col = "grey70")
  points(x_anim[seq_len(k)], rep(-0.004, k),
         pch = 21,
         bg  = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.6),
         col = "steelblue",
         cex = 2.5)

  ## Bottom panel: corresponding Y realizations under f_Y
  curve(f_Y(x),
        from = 0, to = 90,
        ylim = c(-0.004, 0.036),
        xlab = "Jump & Reach (cm)",
        ylab = expression(f[Y](y)),
        lwd  = 2, lty  = 2, col  = "black",
        main = "",
        bty  = "n")
  abline(h = 0, col = "grey70")
  points(y_anim[seq_len(k)], rep(-0.002, k),
         pch = 21,
         bg  = adjustcolor("tomato", alpha.f = 0.6),
         col = "tomato",
         cex = 2.5)

  par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
}
```



::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}

## Summary and Outline

We view the observed data
$$
(Y_{1,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{1,\operatorname{obs}}),\, (Y_{2,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{2,\operatorname{obs}}),\, \ldots,\, (Y_{n,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{n,\operatorname{obs}}),
$$ 
shown in @tbl-class-data, as a realization of $n$ random variable pairs
$$
(Y_{1},\, X_{1}),\, (Y_{2},\, X_{2}),\, \ldots,\, (Y_{n},\, X_{n}).
$$ 

The realizations are randomly drawn from an underlying (typically unknown) distribution(s).

The main tools for characterizing single distributions (for $Y$ or $X$) are: 

* **probability density function (pdf):** @sec-pdf 
* **probability mass function (pmf):** @sec-pmf
* **cumulative distribution function (cdf):** @sec-cdf
* **means and variances:** @sec-moments

The main tools for characterizing connections between distributions (connection between $Y$ and $X$) are: 

* **joint and conditional distributions:** @sec-joint-conditional
* **conditional means (regression):** @sec-regression-function
:::


### Density Function {#sec-pdf}

#### **Continuous Random Variables** {-} 

A random variable, say $X,$ that can take *any* value in an interval $[a,b]$
$$
X\in[a,b]
$$
is called a **continuous random variable** as it takes values in the continuum of real numbers $[a,b]\subset\mathbb{R}.$

Examples of continuous random variables:

* Age $A_i$ taking the values $A_i\geq 0$ [years, days, hours, minutes, seconds, etc.]
* Height $H_i$ taking the values $H_i\geq 0$ [m, cm, mm, etc.]
* Temperature $T_i$ taking values $-\infty < T_i < \infty$ [Degree Celcius]


#### **Density Function** {-}

For a **continuous** random variable $X$ we can describe its distribution most conveniently using the **probability density function (pdf)** $f_X(x).$ 

A **probability density function (pdf)** $f_X(x)$ has the following key properties:

**1. Non-Negativity:**
$$
f_X(x) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all} \quad x
$$
**2. Total Probability:**
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1
$$
**3. General Probabilities:**
The probability that $X$ falls in the interval $[a, b]$ is the area under the density curve over $[a,b]$:
$$
P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_a^b f_X(x)\, dx
$$

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Note that the density value $f_X(x)$ is **not** itself a probability. Only **areas under $f_X$** correspond to probabilities. In fact, $f(x)$ can take values larger than 1.
:::

#### 💻 **Data Generation** {-} 

If the true density is known, we can generate realizations on a the computer. For instance, in case of a normally distributed random variable (@sec-normal) with mean $1.6$ and variance $2,$ we can use the following code:

```{r}
n <- 12 # sample size (number of realizations)

realizations <- rnorm(
    n,            # sample size
    mean = 1.6,   # mean
    sd   = sqrt(2)# standard deviation = square root of variance
)
head(round(realizations, digits = 2)) # first six values
```

#### ⚒️ **Estimation** {-} 

In practice, the true functional form of a density $f_X$ which generates the data is **(typically) unknown**. It's a part of the data-generating process we are trying to learn from data. 


#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

Given a sample of observations 
$$
X_{1,\text{obs}}, \ldots, X_{n,\text{obs}},
$$ 
and 
$$
Y_{1,\text{obs}}, \ldots, Y_{n,\text{obs}},
$$ 
we can estimate the underlying densities $f_X$ and $f_Y$ without assuming specific functional forms. Two standard estimators are:

- The **histogram estimator** divides the range of the data into bins and estimates $f(x)$ by the relative frequency of observations falling into each bin. It is a step function. 
```{r}
#| eval: false
hist(jumptest_data$hairlength, freq = FALSE)# estimates f_X
hist(jumptest_data$jumpreach,  freq = FALSE)# estimates f_Y
``` 
- The **kernel density estimator** places a small symmetric bump (a *kernel*) centered at each observation and sums them up. The result is a smooth curve. 
```{r}
#| eval: false
density(jumptest_data$hairlength) # estimates f_X
density(jumptest_data$jumpreach)  # estimates f_Y
``` 

Both of these (non-parametric) estimators converge to the true $f_X(x)$ and $f_Y(y)$ as $n \to \infty,$ if used correctly. 

@fig-hairlength-hist shows the estimation results based on the observed hair-length data
$$
X_{1,\text{obs}}, \ldots, X_{n,\text{obs}}
$$ 
together with the true **usually unknown** density $f_X$.

```{r}
#| label: fig-hairlength-hist
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Two estimates of the usually unknown density $f_X(x)$ of hair lengths: the histogram estimate (bars) and the kernel density estimate (solid curve). The dashed black curve is the true (usually unknown) density $f_X(x)$."

## Histogram estimation
hist(jumptest_data$hairlength,
     freq     = FALSE,
     col      = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.4),
     border   = "white",
     xlab     = "Hair Length (cm)",
     ylim     = c(0, 0.07),
     main     = "")

## Kernel density estimation
lines(density(jumptest_data$hairlength, adjust = 1.2),
      lwd = 2, col = "steelblue")

curve(f_X(x), add = TRUE, col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("top",
       legend = c("Histogram Estimate",
                  "Kernel Density Estimate",
                  expression(f[X](x)~"(usually unknown)")),
       pch    = c(15, NA, NA),
       lty    = c(NA, 1, 2),
       lwd    = c(NA, 2, 2),
       col    = c(adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.4), "steelblue", "black"),
       bty    = "n",
       y.intersp = 1.3)
```


@fig-jumpreach-hist shows the estimation results based on the observed hair-length data
$$
Y_{1,\text{obs}}, \ldots, Y_{n,\text{obs}}
$$ 
together with the true **usually unknown** density $f_Y$.

```{r}
#| label: fig-jumpreach-hist
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Two estimates of the usually unknown density $f_Y(y)$ of jump and reach scores: the histogram estimate (bars) and the kernel density estimate (solid curve). The dashed black curve is the true (usually unknown) marginal density $f_Y(y)$."

hist(jumptest_data$jumpreach,
     freq   = FALSE,
     col    = adjustcolor("tomato", alpha.f = 0.4),
     border = "white",
     xlab   = "Jump Height (cm)",
     ylim   = c(0, 0.03),
     main   = "")
lines(density(jumptest_data$jumpreach, adjust = 1.2),
      lwd = 2, col = "tomato")
curve(f_Y(x), add = TRUE, col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("top",
       legend = c("Histogram Estimate",
                  "Kernel Density Estimate",
                  expression(f[Y](y)~"(usually unknown)")),
       pch    = c(15, NA, NA),
       lty    = c(NA, 1, 2),
       lwd    = c(NA, 2, 2),
       col    = c(adjustcolor("tomato", alpha.f = 0.4), "tomato", "black"),
       bty    = "n",
       y.intersp = 1.3)
```


::: {.callout-note}
# Summary: Density Function

* 🤓 The probability density funciton (pdf) or short **density** allows describing the distribution of **continuous random variables**.
<br> 
* 🙂 Densities are particularly intuitive to interpret: The realizations of a random variable tend to cluster at regions of high density.
<br> 
* 🙁 As long as we only look at ***marginal* densities** $f_X(x)$ or $f_Y(x),$ we cannot see a possible connection between the random variables $X$ and $Y.$ To see connections, we need 
   * ***joint* densities** $f_{YX}$ (@sec-joint-conditional) or
   * **conditional means (regression)** (@sec-regression-function). 
<br>
* ⚒️ **Non-parametric estimation:** To estimate an unknown density function $f_X(x)$ we can use for instance a histogram or a kernel density estimator.
    * 🙂 Require no functional form assumption
    * 🙁 Inefficient estimation 
<br>
* ⚒️ **Parametric estimation:** If you know the general functional form of $f_X(x)$ you can improve the estimation result. For instance, if $f_X(x)$ is the density of a normal distribution, but you do not know the mean and variance parameters, you can focus on estimating these unknown parameters instead of estimating the total functional form; see @sec-sec-normal.
    * 🙁 Requires a functional form assumption
    * 🙂 Efficient estimation    
:::

### Probability Mass Function {#sec-pmf}

#### **Discrete Random Variables** {-} 

A random variable, say $W,$ that takes only **countably many values** such as 
$$
W_i \in\{w_1, w_2, \ldots\},
$$
is called a **discrete random variable.** 

Examples of discrete random variables:

* Gender[^1] $G_i\in\{\texttt{female}, \text{male}\}$
* Rolling a die $D_i\in\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$
* Eye color $C_i\in\{\texttt{blue}, \texttt{brown}, \texttt{hazel}, \texttt{green}, \texttt{gray}\}$
* Number of coin flips required to get the first head, $N_i\in\{1,2,\dots\}$

[^1]: In practice, gender and biological sex are measured in various ways depending on the research context. This example uses a binary coding for simplicity; real datasets may include additional categories or continuous measures.

For discrete random variable a density function does *not* exist. There is no smooth curve whose area gives probabilities, because all probability is concentrated on isolated points.

#### **Probability Mass Function** {-}

For a **discrete** random variable 
$$
W\in\{w_1,w_2,\dots\}
$$ 
we describe its distribution using the **probability mass function (pmf)** 
$$
p_W(w_k) = P(W = w_k)\quad\text{for all}\quad k=1,2,\dots
$$ 
The pmf has the following three key properties:

**1. Non-Negativity:**
$$
p_W(w_k) \geq 0\quad\text{for all}\quad k=1,2,\dots
$$
**2. Total Probability:**
$$
\sum_{k=1,2,\ldots} p_W(w_k) = 1
$$
**3. General Probabilities:**
$$
p_W(w_k) = P(W = w_k), \quad\text{for all}\quad k = 1, 2, \ldots
$$

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Unlike the density $f_X(x)$, the value $p_W(w_k)$ **is** itself a probability — the probability that $W$ realizes itself to the value $w_k.$
:::


#### 💻 **Data Generation** {-} 

If the true pmf is known, we can generate realizations on the computer. For instance, to generate realization of a random gender variable with two gender-outcomes 
$$
G_i\in\{\texttt{female}, \text{male}\}
$$
we can use the following code:

```{r}
n <- 11 # sample size (number of die rolls)

gender_realizations <- sample(
    c("female", "male"),  # possible outcome values 
    size    = n,          # sample size
    replace = TRUE,       # values can occure more than once
    prob    = c(1/2, 1/2) # probabilities for each outcome value
)
head(gender_realizations) # first six values
```


#### ⚒️ **Estimation** {-} 

In practice, we typically know the values $w_1,w_2,\dots$ a discrete random variable $W$ can take, but the corresponding probabilities $p_W$ are **(typically) unknown**. Given the observed realizations 
$$
W_{1,\operatorname{obs}}, \ldots, W_{n,\operatorname{obs}},
$$ 
we estimate the values of the pmf $p_W(w_k)$ using **relative frequencies**:
\begin{align*}
\hat{p}_{W,\operatorname{obs}}(w_k) 
&= \frac{\text{number of observations equal to } w_k}{n}\\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\mathbf{1}(W_{i,\text{obs}} = w_k)\quad\text{for all}\quad k = 1, 2, \ldots,
\end{align*}
where $\mathbf{1}(\texttt{TRUE})=1$ and $\mathbf{1}(\texttt{FALSE})=0$ is the indicator function. 

#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

Let's say a third variable was recorded alongside hair length and jump and reach: a **gender indicator** $G_i$ called `gender` taking the value 

* $G_i = \texttt{male}$ if student $i$ is a male student 
* $G_i = \texttt{female}$ if student $i$ is a female student

@tbl-gender-data shows the first six values of the observed gender variable data
$$
G_{1,\text{obs}},\dots,G_{n,\text{obs}}
$$

```{r}
#| label: tbl-gender-data
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "First six values of the observed gender variable."
kbl(head(gender),
    col.names = c("Gender"),
    align = c("r", "r")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```



Since $G_i$ takes only two values $\{\texttt{male},\texttt{female}\},$ it is a **discrete random variable**, and its distribution is characterized by the (unknown) pmf values $p_G(\texttt{male})$ and $p_G(\texttt{female}).$

@fig-gender-pmf shows the estimation results
\begin{align*}
\hat{p}_{G,\operatorname{obs}}(\texttt{male})
&= \frac{\text{number of observations equal to } \texttt{male}}{n}\\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\mathbf{1}(G_{i,\text{obs}} = \texttt{male})\\[4pt]
&= \texttt{sum(gender == "male") / length(gender)}\\[4pt]
&= `r round(sum(jumptest_data$gender == "male") / length(jumptest_data$gender), 2)`
\end{align*}

and

\begin{align*}
\hat{p}_{G,\operatorname{obs}}(\texttt{female})
&= \frac{\text{number of observations equal to } \texttt{female}}{n}\\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\mathbf{1}(G_{i,\text{obs}} = \texttt{female})\\[4pt]
&= \texttt{sum(gender == "female") / length(gender)}\\[4pt]
&= `r round(sum(jumptest_data$gender == "female") / length(jumptest_data$gender), 2)`
\end{align*}
<br>
together with the **usually unknown** true pmf values
$$
p_G(\texttt{male}) = p_G(\texttt{female}) = 0.5.
$$

```{r}
#| label: fig-gender-pmf
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Estimated probability mass function values $\\hat{p}_{G,\\operatorname{obs}}(\texttt{male})$ and $\\hat{p}_{G,\\operatorname{obs}}(\texttt{female})$ (purple bars). The dashed black line is the true pmf $p_G(\texttt{male}) = p_G(\texttt{female}) = 0.5$."

gender_probs <- as.numeric(table(jumptest_data$gender)) / length(jumptest_data$gender)

bp <- barplot(gender_probs,
              names.arg = c("G = female", "G = male"),
              col       = adjustcolor("purple", alpha.f = 0.5),
              border    = "white",
              ylab      = "",
              ylim      = c(0, 0.8),
              main      = "")
abline(h = 0.5, col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = c(expression(hat(p)["G,obs"]), expression(p[G](g) == 0.5)),
       pch    = c(15, NA),
       lty    = c(NA, 2),
       lwd    = c(NA, 2),
       col    = c(adjustcolor("purple", alpha.f = 0.5), "black"),
       bty    = "n")
```


::: {.callout-note}
# Summary: Probability Mass Function

* 🤓 The probability mass funciton (pmf) allows describing the distribution of **discrete random variables**. 
<br> 
* 🙂 Probability mass functions are to interpret: The realizations of a discrete random variable tend to take values that have a high probability. 
<br> 
* 🙁 As long as we only look at ***marginal* pmfs**, we cannot see a possible connections between two or more random variables. 
<br>
* ⚒️ **Non-parametric estimation:** To estimate an unknown pmf we can simply use relative frequencies.
:::


### Cumulative Distribution Function {#sec-cdf}

The **cumulative distribution function (cdf)** or short **distribution function** $F_X(x)$ is defined as 
$$
F_X(x) = P(X \leq x).
$$

This definition works for any random variable, continuous, discrete, and even hybrid forms of random variables that are partly continuous and discrete. 

$F_X(x)$ tells us the probability that $X$ takes a value at most $x$. It has the following properties:

**1. $F_X(x)$ is Non-Decreasing:** 
$$
x_1 < x_2 \Rightarrow F_X(x_1) \leq F_X(x_2)
$$
**2. $F_X(x)$ is Normalized:**
$$
\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0\quad\text{and}\quad \lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) = 1.
$$
**3. $F(x)$ is Right-Continuous:**
$$
\lim_{x \to c+} F_X(x) = F_X(c),
$$
where $x \to c+$ means that $x$ goes to $c$ from the "right", i.e. coming from values larger than $c$.

**4. For continuous $X$:** 
$$
F_X(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^x f_X(x)dx.
$$
and
$$
f_X(x) = F'_X(x),
$$ 
where $F'_X(x)$ denotes the first derivative of $F_X(x).$



#### ⚒️ **Estimation** {-} 

In practice, the true cdf is **(typically) unknown**. Given the observed values, we estimate it with the **empirical cdf**, defined as the fraction of observations at most $x$:

\begin{align*}
\widehat{F}_{\operatorname{obs}}(x) 
& = \frac{\text{number of observations equal to or smaller than $x$}}{n}\\
& = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}(X_{i,\operatorname{obs}} \leq x),
\end{align*}
where $\mathbf{1}(\texttt{TRUE})=1$ and $\mathbf{1}(\texttt{FALSE})=0$ is the indicator function. 

The empirical cdf $\widehat{F}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ is a step function that converges to the true cdf $F$ as $n \to \infty$. 


In R, `ecdf()` computes the empirical cdf $\widehat{F}_{\operatorname{obs}}(x).$


#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

@fig-hairlength-ecdf shows 
$$
\widehat{F}_{X,\operatorname{obs}}  = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}(X_{i,\operatorname{obs}} \leq x),
$$ 
based on the observed hair-length data
$$
X_{1,\text{obs}}, \ldots, X_{n,\text{obs}}
$$ 
together with the true **usually unknown** cdf $F_X$.


```{r}
#| label: fig-hairlength-ecdf
#| fig-cap: "Empirical cdf $\\widehat{F}_{X,\\operatorname{obs}}(x)$ of hair lengths (solid blue step function). The dashed black curve is the true cdf $F_X(x)$."

plot(ecdf(jumptest_data$hairlength),
     xlab      = "Hair Length (cm)",
     ylab      = expression(hat(F)["X,obs"](x)),
     main      = "",
     col       = "steelblue",
     lwd       = 2,
     verticals = TRUE,
     do.points = FALSE)
curve(F_X(x), add = TRUE, col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("topleft",
       legend = c(expression(hat(F)["X,obs"](x)), expression(F[X](x))),
       col    = c("steelblue", "black"),
       lty    = c(1, 2), lwd = 2, bty = "n")
```


@fig-jumpreach-ecdf shows 
$$
\widehat{F}_{Y,\operatorname{obs}}  = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}(Y_{i,\operatorname{obs}} \leq x),
$$ 
based on the observed jump and reach data
$$
Y_{1,\text{obs}}, \ldots, Y_{n,\text{obs}}
$$ 
together with the true **usually unknown** cdf $F_Y$.


```{r}
#| label: fig-jumpreach-ecdf
#| fig-cap: "Empirical cdf $\\widehat{F}_{Y,\\operatorname{obs}}(y)$ of jump and reach scores (solid red step function). The dashed black curve is the true marginal cdf $F_Y(y)$."

plot(ecdf(jumptest_data$jumpreach),
     xlab      = "Jump Height (cm)",
     ylab      = expression(hat(F)["Y,obs"](y)),
     main      = "",
     col       = "tomato",
     lwd       = 2,
     verticals = TRUE,
     do.points = FALSE)
curve(F_Y(x), add = TRUE, col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("topleft",
       legend = c(expression(hat(F)["Y,obs"](y)), expression(F[Y](y))),
       col    = c("tomato", "black"),
       lty    = c(1, 2), lwd = 2, bty = "n")
```




::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
# Summary: Distribution Function

* 🤓 The cdf is well-defined for both continuous and discrete, and even for  **mixtures** of continuous and discrete random variables. The definition 
$$
F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)
$$ 
requires nothing more than a probability, and probabilities are always defined.
<br> 
* 🙁 As long as we only look at ***marginal* cdfs**, we cannot see a possible connections between two or more random variables. 
<br>
* ⚒️ **Non-parametric estimation:** To estimate an unknown cdf we can simply use relative frequencies.
:::

## Mean and Variance {#sec-moments}

Two following two parameters summarize a distribution more compactly than the full density: 

* **Mean:** The **expected value** or **mean** tells us something about the center (i.e. its location) of a distribution
* **Variance:** The **variance** tells us something about how much the data spreads out around its mean.

❗ Mean (location) and variance (spread) are only important features of a distribution. Knowing both parameters does *not* give us the complete functional form of a distribution. 

### Mean Value {-}

The **mean** or **expected value** of a continuous random variable $X$ with density $f_X$ is
$$
\mu_X = E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\, f_X(x)\, dx.
$$

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Think of $E(X)$ as the long-run average: if you drew from the distribution $f_X$ *many* times and averaged the results, the average would converge to $E(X)$ when averaging infinitely many results.
:::

For a discrete random variable $W$ with possible values $w_1, w_2, \ldots$ and probabilities $p_W(w_k) = P(W = w_k)$, the integral becomes a sum:
$$
\mu_W = E(W) = \sum_{k=1,2,\dots} w_k\, p_W(w_k).
$$

❗ The mean of a discrete random variable 
$$
W\in\{w_1,w_2,\dots\}
$$ 
only makes sense in cases where the values $w_1,w_2,\dots$ are numeric. 

**Example:** The expected value when rolling a die 
$$
D\in\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}
$$
is
\begin{align*}
E(D) 
& = 1 \cdot  \frac{1}{6} +  2 \cdot\frac{1}{6} + 3\cdot\frac{1}{6} + 4 \cdot\frac{1}{6} + 5 \cdot\frac{1}{6}+ 6 \cdot\frac{1}{6}\\
& = \frac{1}{6}\cdot(1+2+3+4+5+6)\\
& = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^6 k \\
& = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{6\cdot(6+1)}{2} = 3.5
\end{align*}
where $\sum_{k=1}^6 k = \frac{6\cdot(6+1)}{2}$ follows from the [Gaussian summation formula](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gau%C3%9Fsche_Summenformel).


#### **Properties of the Expected Value** {-}

The expected value is *linear*; i.e. for constants $a$ and $b$,
$$
E(aX + b) = a\, E(X) + b.
$$

For two random variables $X$ and $Y$ (not necessarily independent of each other):
$$
E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y).
$$


#### ⚒️ **Estimation** {-} 

In practice, the true density $f_X$ and the true pmf $p_W$ are typically unknown — so neither formula can be evaluated directly to compute the true means $E(Y)$ and $E(W).$ 

Given observed realizations of a continuous random variable
$$
X_{1,\text{obs}},\dots,X_{n,\text{obs}}
$$
we can estimate $E(X)$ by 
$$
\bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n X_{i,\operatorname{obs}}.
$$
Alternative notations: 
$$
\bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}} = \hat{\mu}_{X,\operatorname{obs}} = \widehat{E}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X)
$$

Given observed realizations of a discrete random variable
$$
W_{1,\text{obs}},\dots,W_{n,\text{obs}}
$$
we can estimate $E(W)$ by 
$$
\bar{W}_{\operatorname{obs}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n W_{i,\operatorname{obs}}.
$$
Alternative notations: 
$$
\bar{W}_{\operatorname{obs}} = \hat{\mu}_{W,\operatorname{obs}} = \widehat{E}_{\operatorname{obs}}(W)
$$

❗ Estimating the mean of a discrete random variable 
$$
W\in\{w_1,w_2,\dots\}
$$ 
only makes sense in cases where the values $w_1,w_2,\dots$ are numeric (e.g. rolling dice). 

#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

Given observed data
$$
X_{1,\operatorname{obs}}, \ldots, X_{n,\operatorname{obs}}
$$
and
$$
Y_{1,\operatorname{obs}}, \ldots, Y_{n,\operatorname{obs}}
$$
we can estimate the expected values $E(X)$ and $E(Y)$ by the **sample means**:
$$
\bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n X_{i,\operatorname{obs}}
$$
$$
\bar{Y}_{\operatorname{obs}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n Y_{i,\operatorname{obs}}
$$

In R, `mean()` computes sample means:

* $\bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}}=$`mean(jumptest_data$hairlength)` $=$ `r round(mean(jumptest_data$hairlength),1)`
* $\bar{Y}_{\operatorname{obs}}=$`mean(jumptest_data$jumpreach)` $=$ `r round(mean(jumptest_data$jumpreach),1)`

Since we generated the data ourselves, we also know the true population means (usually unknown in practice):

* $E(X)=$ `r 0.5*mu_X_m + 0.5*mu_X_f`
* $E(Y)=$ `r 0.5*mu_Y_m + 0.5*mu_Y_f`

So, the estimates $\bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ and $\bar{Y}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ are doing a good job - both have values close to the true (usually unknown) values.

### Variance and Standard Deviation {-}

The **variance** of a continuous random variable $X$ with density $f_X$ is the expected squared deviation from its mean:

\begin{align*}
\sigma_X^2 = \text{Var}(X)
&= E\!\left[(X - \mu_X)^2\right]\\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu_X)^2\, f_X(x)\, dx.
\end{align*}

The **standard deviation** is
$$
\sigma_X = \text{SD}(X) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)},
$$
which has the same units as $X$ and is therefore easier to interpret.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Think of $\text{Var}(X)$ as the average squared distance of a draw from the mean. A large variance means draws tend to spread far from $\mu_X$; a small variance means they cluster tightly around it. The standard deviation $\text{SD}(X)$ expresses that same spread on the original scale of $X$.
:::

For a discrete random variable $W$ with possible values $w_1, w_2, \ldots$ and probabilities $p_W(w_k) = P(W = w_k)$, the integral becomes a sum:

\begin{align*}
\sigma_W^2 = \text{Var}(W)
&= E\!\left[(W - \mu_W)^2\right]\\
&= \sum_{k=1,2,\dots} (w_k - \mu_W)^2\, p_W(w_k).
\end{align*}

**Example:** The variance when rolling a fair die $D \in \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, where $E(D) = 3.5$, is
\begin{align*}
\text{Var}(D)
&= (1-3.5)^2\cdot\tfrac{1}{6} + (2-3.5)^2\cdot\tfrac{1}{6} + (3-3.5)^2\cdot\tfrac{1}{6}+\\
&+ (4-3.5)^2\cdot\tfrac{1}{6} + (5-3.5)^2\cdot\tfrac{1}{6} + (6-3.5)^2\cdot\tfrac{1}{6}\\
&= \frac{1}{6}\cdot\big[(1-3.5)^2 + (2-3.5)^2 + (3-3.5)^2 \\
&\phantom{\frac{1}{6}\cdot} + (4-3.5)^2 + (5-3.5)^2 + (6-3.5)^2\big]\\
&= \frac{1}{6}\cdot\left[\frac{25}{4} + \frac{9}{4} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{9}{4} + \frac{25}{4}\right]\\
&= \frac{1}{6}\cdot\frac{25+9+1+1+9+25}{4}\\
&= \frac{35}{12} \approx 2.92,
\end{align*}
so the standard deviation is $\text{SD}(D) = \sqrt{35/12} \approx 1.71$.

#### **Properties of Variance and Standard Deviation** {-}

Variance scales **quadratically** with the scale factor and is unaffected by constant shifts:
$$
\text{Var}(aX + b) = a^2\,\text{Var}(X).
$$
Standard deviation scales **linearly** and is likewise unaffected by constant shifts:
$$
\text{SD}(aX + b) = |a|\,\text{SD}(X).
$$

#### ⚒️ **Estimation** {-}

In practice, the true density $f_X$ is typically unknown — so neither $\text{Var}(X)$ nor $\text{SD}(X)$ can be evaluated directly.

Given observed realizations
$$
X_{1,\operatorname{obs}}, \ldots, X_{n,\operatorname{obs}},
$$
we estimate $\text{Var}(X)$ by the **sample variance**
$$
\hat{\sigma}^2_{X,\operatorname{obs}} = \widehat{\text{Var}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X) = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n \bigl(X_{i,\operatorname{obs}} - \bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}}\bigr)^2,
$$
and $\text{SD}(X)$ by the **sample standard deviation**
$$
\hat{\sigma}_{X,\operatorname{obs}} = \sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2_{X,\operatorname{obs}}}.
$$
The divisor $n-1$ rather than $n$ corrects for the fact that $\bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ is itself estimated from the same data.

#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

In R, `var()` computes the sample variance and `sd()` computes the sample standard deviation:

* $\hat\sigma_{X,\operatorname{obs}}^2=$ `var(jumptest_data$hairlength)` $=$ `r round(var(jumptest_data$hairlength),1)`
* $\hat\sigma_{X,\operatorname{obs}}=$ `sd(jumptest_data$hairlength)` $=$ `r round(sd(jumptest_data$hairlength),1)`
* $\hat\sigma_{Y,\operatorname{obs}}^2=$ `var(jumptest_data$jumpreach)` $=$ `r round(var(jumptest_data$jumpreach),1)`
* $\hat\sigma_{Y,\operatorname{obs}}=$ `sd(jumptest_data$jumpreach)` $=$ `r round(sd(jumptest_data$jumpreach),1)`

Since we generated the data ourselves, we also know the true population variances and standard deviations (usually unknown in practice):

* $\sigma_X^2=$ `r round(0.5*sigma_X_m^2 + 0.5*sigma_X_f^2 + (mu_X_m-mu_X_f)^2/4, 1)`
* $\sigma_X=$ `r round(sqrt(0.5*sigma_X_m^2 + 0.5*sigma_X_f^2 + (mu_X_m-mu_X_f)^2/4), 1)`
* $\sigma_Y^2=$ `r round(0.5*sigma_Y_m^2 + 0.5*sigma_Y_f^2 + (mu_Y_m-mu_Y_f)^2/4, 1)`
* $\sigma_Y=$ `r round(sqrt(0.5*sigma_Y_m^2 + 0.5*sigma_Y_f^2 + (mu_Y_m-mu_Y_f)^2/4), 1)`


## Joint and Conditional Distributions {#sec-joint-conditional}

Most interesting statistical questions involve two or more random variables. Often, we want to understand how they vary together.

### Bivariate Density Function {-}

For two **continuous** random variables, the joint distribution is characterized by the **bivariate probability density function (bivariate pdf)** $f_{Y,X}(y, x)$, which has the following key properties:

**1. Non-Negativity:**
$$
f_{Y,X}(y, x) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } (y, x)
$$
**2. Total Probability:**
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{Y,X}(y, x)\, dy\, dx = 1.
$$
**3. General Probabilities:**
$$
P\!\left((Y, X) \in [a_Y,b_Y]\times[a_X,b_X]\right) = \int_{a_Y}^{b_Y}\int_{a_X}^{b_X} f_{Y,X}(y, x)\, dx\, dy.
$$
That is, the probability that a realization of $(Y, X)$ falls in the rectangular region $[a_Y,b_Y]\times[a_X,b_X]$ of the plane equals the volume under the density surface over that region.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Note that $f_{Y,X}(y, x)$ is **not** itself a probability — it is a density. Only volumes under $f_{Y,X}$ correspond to probabilities. In fact, $f_{Y,X}(y,x)$ can take values larger than 1.
:::


### Marginal Densities Functions {-}

The **marginal densities** $f_Y$ and $f_X$ are recovered from $f_{Y,X}$ by integrating out the other variable:

$$
f_Y(y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{Y,X}(y, x)\, dx
$$
$$
f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{Y,X}(y, x)\, dy
$$

Intuitively, $f_X$ is what you get if you ignore $Y$ entirely and look only at the distribution of $X$ — exactly the univariate density we considered in @sec-pdf.

The **bivariate cdf** records the probability that both components fall below their respective thresholds simultaneously:

$$
F_{Y,X}(y, x) = P(Y \leq y,\; X \leq x)
= \int_{-\infty}^{y}\int_{-\infty}^{x} f_{Y,X}(t, s)\, ds\, dt.
$$

If $X$ and $Y$ are **independent** (short notation: $Y \perp X$), knowing the value of one tells us nothing about the other. Independence is equivalent to the joint density factoring into the product of the marginals:

$$
Y \perp X \;\Longleftrightarrow\; f_{Y,X}(y,x) = f_Y(y)\cdot f_X(x),
$$
which in turn implies 
$$
Y \perp X \;\Longleftrightarrow\; F_{Y,X}(y,x) = F_Y(y)\cdot F_X(x).
$$

From the scatter plot and the contours in @fig-joint-kde, we see that the cloud of points is not circular — high values of $X$ (hair length) tend to accompany low values of $Y$ (jump and reach scores). The joint distribution of $Y$ and $X$ is therefore not the same as drawing $Y$ and $X$ independently.



#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

In practice, the true bivariate density $f_{Y,X}$, which generates the data, is **(typically) unknown**. Given the observed pairs
$$
(Y_{1,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{1,\operatorname{obs}}), \ldots, (Y_{n,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{n,\operatorname{obs}}),
$$
we can estimate $f_{Y,X}$ non-parametrically using, for instance, the bivariate kernel density estimator. 

The **bivariate kernel density estimator** extends the univariate kernel estimator to two dimensions: it places a two-dimensional symmetric bump at each observed pair and sums them up, producing a smooth surface over the plane. In R, `MASS::kde2d()` computes the bivariate kernel desntiy estimate from given data; `contour()` overlays its level curves on a scatter plot.

```{r}
#| label: fig-joint-kde
#| fig-cap: "Scatter plot with contours of the bivariate kernel density estimate (solid red) and of the true (usually unknown) joint density $f_{Y,X}$ (dashed black)."

kde <- MASS::kde2d(jumptest_data$jumpreach, jumptest_data$hairlength, n = 50)

## True joint density on a grid (x-axis = jumpreach = Y, y-axis = hairlength = X)
push_grid <- seq(0, 80, length.out = 80)
hair_grid <- seq(0, 50, length.out = 80)
true_z    <- outer(push_grid, hair_grid,
                   function(push, hair) f_YgX(push, hair) * f_X(hair))

plot(jumptest_data$jumpreach, jumptest_data$hairlength,
     xlab = "Jump Height (cm)",
     ylab = "Hair Length (cm)",
     pch  = 19,
     col  = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.6))
contour(kde, add = TRUE, col = "tomato", lwd = 1.5)
contour(list(x = push_grid, y = hair_grid, z = true_z),
        add = TRUE, col = "black", lwd = 1.5, lty = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = c("Kernel Density Estimate",
                  expression(f["Y,X"])),
       col    = c("tomato", "black"),
       lty    = c(1, 2), lwd = 1.5, bty = "n")
```



### Covariance and Correlation {-}

Two summary statistics

* covariance and 
* correlation

describe how two random variables move together.

The **covariance** is

$$
\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E\!\left[(X - E(X))(Y - E(Y))\right].
$$

If large values of $X$ tend to go with large values of $Y$, the covariance is positive. If large values of $X$ go with small values of $Y$, it is negative (as in the jump-test example).

Note that the covariance of $X$ with itself is just the variance of $X\colon$
\begin{align*}
\text{Cov}(X, X) 
&= E\!\left[(X - E(X))(X - E(X))\right]\\ 
&= E\!\left[(X - E(X))^2\right]\\
&=\text{Var}(X).
\end{align*}
The covariance is symmetric:
$$
\text{Cov}(Y,X) = \text{Cov}(X,Y)
$$
Moreover, following calculus rule is often useful:
$$
\text{Cov}(a_X X + b_X, a_Y Y + b_Y) = a_X a_Y \text{Cov}(X,Y) 
$$

The **correlation** standardizes the covariance to lie in $[-1, 1]$:

$$
\text{Cor}(X, Y) = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(X)\cdot\text{Var}(Y)}}.
$$

A correlation of $+1$ or $-1$ implies a perfect positive or negative linear relationship. A correlation of $0$ implies no linear association (though a non-linear relationship may still exist).

In practice, the true $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$ and $\text{Cor}(X, Y)$ are **(typically) unknown** — they depend on the true joint distribution $f_{Y,X}$, which we do not observe.

#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

Given observed data
$$
(X_{1,\operatorname{obs}},\, Y_{1,\operatorname{obs}}), \ldots, (X_{n,\operatorname{obs}},\, Y_{n,\operatorname{obs}}),
$$
we can estimate them by their **sample counterparts**:

$$
\widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X, Y) = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n
  (X_{i,\operatorname{obs}} - \bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}})
  (Y_{i,\operatorname{obs}} - \bar{Y}_{\operatorname{obs}}),
$$

$$
\widehat{\text{Cor}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X, Y)
= \frac{\widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X, Y)}{\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2_{X,\operatorname{obs}}\, \hat{\sigma}^2_{Y,\operatorname{obs}}}},
$$
where $\hat{\sigma}^2_{X,\operatorname{obs}}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2_{Y,\operatorname{obs}}$ are the sample standard deviations of $X$ and $Y$ respectively. 


In R, `cov()` and `cor()` can be used to compute the empirical (i.e. data based) estimates $\widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X,Y)$ and $\widehat{\text{Cor}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X,Y)$:

$\widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X,Y)=$ `cov(hairlength, jumpreach)` $=$ $`r round(cov(jumptest_data$hairlength, jumptest_data$jumpreach), 2)`$

$\widehat{\text{Cor}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X,Y)=$ `cor(hairlength, jumpreach)` $=$ $`r round(cor(jumptest_data$hairlength, jumptest_data$jumpreach), 2)`$



### Conditional Distribution {-}

The **conditional distribution** of $Y$ given $X = x$ is the distribution of jump and reach scores among all students whose hair length is exactly $x$ cm. It answers the question: once we know that $X_{\operatorname{obs}} = x$, what does the distribution of the jump and reach score $Y$ look like?

For two continuous random variables with joint density $f_{Y,X}(y, x)$ and marginal density $f_X(x) > 0,$ the **conditional density of $Y$ given $X = x$** is

$$
f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x) = \frac{f_{Y,X}(y,\, x)}{f_X(x)}.
$$

For fixed $x$, $f_{Y \mid X}(\,\cdot \mid x)$ is a proper density in $y$: it is non-negative and integrates to 1. Intuitively, it is the cross-section of the joint density surface $f_{Y,X}$ at height $X = x$, rescaled so that the area under the slice equals 1.

The definition makes the link between joint and marginal densities concrete. Rearranging gives the **multiplication rule**:

$$
f_{Y,X}(y,x) = f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x)\cdot f_X(x).
$$

If $Y$ and $X$ are independent, we have that
$$
f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x) = f_Y(y).
$$
That is, knowing $x$ tells us nothing about the distribution of $Y.$

#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

We can get a feel for the conditional distribution from data by splitting the observed pairs
$$
(X_{1,\operatorname{obs}}, Y_{1,\operatorname{obs}}), \ldots, (X_{n,\operatorname{obs}}, Y_{n,\operatorname{obs}})
$$
into groups by hair length and looking at the jump and reach distribution within each group.

```{r}
#| label: fig-conditional-hists
#| fig-cap: "Conditional distribution of jump and reach scores for three hair-length groups. The dashed black curve in each panel shows the true (usually unknown) conditional density $f_{Y\\mid X}(y\\mid x)$ evaluated at the within-group mean hair lengths $x$."
#| fig-width: 10

grp1 <- jumptest_data$jumpreach[jumptest_data$hairlength <= 10]
grp2 <- jumptest_data$jumpreach[
  jumptest_data$hairlength > 10 & jumptest_data$hairlength <= 25]
grp3 <- jumptest_data$jumpreach[jumptest_data$hairlength >  25]

## Within-group mean hair length (conditioning point for true density)
group_mid <- c(
  mean(jumptest_data$hairlength[jumptest_data$hairlength <= 10]),
  mean(jumptest_data$hairlength[
    jumptest_data$hairlength > 10 & jumptest_data$hairlength <= 25]),
  mean(jumptest_data$hairlength[jumptest_data$hairlength >  25]))

par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
hist(grp1,
     freq   = FALSE,
     main   = "Hair in [0 cm, 10 cm]",
     xlab   = "Jump Height (cm)",
     col    = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.5),
     border = "white",
     xlim   = c(0, 80),
     ylim   = c(0, 0.12))
curve(f_YgX(x, group_mid[1]), add = TRUE, col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2)

hist(grp2,
     freq   = FALSE,
     main   = "Hair in (10 cm, 25 cm]",
     xlab   = "Jump Height (cm)",
     col    = adjustcolor("purple", alpha.f = 0.5),
     border = "white",
     xlim   = c(0, 80),
     ylim   = c(0, 0.12))
curve(f_YgX(x, group_mid[2]), add = TRUE, col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2)

hist(grp3,
     freq   = FALSE,
     main   = "Hair > 25 cm",
     xlab   = "Jump Height (cm)",
     col    = adjustcolor("tomato", alpha.f = 0.5),
     border = "white",
     xlim   = c(0, 80),
     ylim   = c(0, 0.12))
curve(f_YgX(x, group_mid[3]), add = TRUE, col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

The three histograms in @fig-conditional-hists have clearly different centers: the conditional distribution of jump and reach scores shifts downward as hair length increases. This is the conditional distribution $f_{Y|X}(y|x)$ in action.

### More than two Variables {-}

In regression analysis (@sec-linearRegCh) we typically have one outcome $Y$ and $K$ predictors $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_K$. All concepts from the bivariate case extend naturally.

**Joint distribution.** The joint density $f_{Y, X_1, \ldots, X_K}(y, x_1, \ldots, x_K)$ satisfies the same three key properties:

**1. Non-Negativity:**
$$
f_{Y, X_1, \ldots, X_K}(y, x_1, \ldots, x_K) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } (y, x_1, \ldots, x_K)
$$
**2. Total Probability:**
$$
\int \cdots \int f_{Y, X_1, \ldots, X_K}(y, x_1, \ldots, x_K)\, dx_K\, \cdots\, dx_1 \cdots dy = 1.
$$
**3. General Probabilities:**
\begin{align*}
&P\!\left((Y, X) \in [a_Y,b_Y]\times[a_{X_1},b_{X_1}]\times\dots\times[a_{X_K},b_{X_K}]\right)\\
&= \int_{a_Y}^{b_Y}\int_{a_{X_1}}^{b_{X_1}}\dots\int_{a_{X_K}}^{b_{X_K}}  f_{Y,X_1,\dots,X_k}(y, x_1,\dots,x_K)\, dx_K\dots dx_1 dy.
\end{align*}
That is, the probability that a realization of $(Y, X_1, \ldots, X_K)$ falls into the rectangular region $[a_Y,b_Y]\times[a_{X_1},b_{X_1}]\times\dots\times[a_{X_K},b_{X_K}]$ equals the integral of $f_{Y, X_1, \ldots, X_K}$ over that region.

The joint cdf is $F_{Y, X_1, \ldots, X_K}(y, x_1, \ldots, x_K) = P(Y \leq y,\, X_1 \leq x_1,\, \ldots,\, X_K \leq x_K)$.


#### **Covariance and Correlation Matrix** {-}

With $K+1$ variables, pairwise covariances are collected in the $(K+1)\times (K+1)$ **covariance matrix**

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
\text{Var}(Y_1)      & \text{Cov}(Y_1,X_1)  & \cdots & \text{Cov}(Y_1,X_K) \\
\text{Cov}(X_1,Y_1)  & \text{Var}(X_1)      & \cdots & \text{Cov}(X_1,X_K) \\
\vdots               & \vdots               & \ddots & \vdots \\
\text{Cov}(X_K,Y_1)  & \text{Cov}(X_K,X_1)  & \cdots & \text{Var}(X_K)
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

Standardizing each entry by the product of the corresponding standard deviations yields the **correlation matrix**: 
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
1                    & \text{Cor}(Y_1,X_1)  & \cdots & \text{Cor}(Y_1,X_K) \\
\text{Cor}(X_1,Y_1)  & 1                    & \cdots & \text{Cor}(X_1,X_K) \\
\vdots               & \vdots               & \ddots & \vdots \\
\text{Cor}(X_K,Y_1)  & \text{Cor}(X_K,X_1)  & \cdots & 1
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

Typically, we do not know the covariance matrix, and thus also not the correlation matrix. In R, `cov()` and `cor()` accept a data frame or matrix and return the sample versions 

#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

Let denote the observed hair length data by
$$
X_{i,1,\text{obs}}\quad i=1,2,\dots
$$
and the observed gender data by 
$$
X_{i,2,\text{obs}}\quad i=1,2,\dots,
$$
where 
$$
X_{i,2,\text{obs}} = 0\quad\text{if person $i$ is male}
$$
$$
X_{i,2,\text{obs}} = 1\quad\text{if person $i$ is female}
$$

```{r}
#| echo: false
kbl(head(jumptest_data[,c(1,2,3)]),
    col.names = c("Y: Jump Reach (cm)", "X1: Hair Length (cm)", "X2: Gender (1 = female, 0 = male)"),
    align = c("r", "r")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

The estimate of the covariance matrix
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
\widehat{\text{Var}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(Y)     & \widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(Y,X_1)& \widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(Y,X_2)\\ 
\widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_1,Y) & \widehat{\text{Var}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_1)  & \widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_1,X_2)\\
\widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_2,Y) & \widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_2,X_1)  & \widehat{\text{Var}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_2)
\end{pmatrix}.
$$
can be computed by 

```{r}
cov(cbind(jumpreach, hairlength, gender01)) |> 
round(digits = 2)
```
and
$$
\begin{pmatrix}
1     & \widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(Y,X_1)& \widehat{\text{Cor}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(Y,X_2)\\ 
\widehat{\text{Cor}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_1,Y) & 1  & \widehat{\text{Cov}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_1,X_2)\\
\widehat{\text{Cor}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_2,Y) & \widehat{\text{Cor}}_{\operatorname{obs}}(X_2,X_1)  & 1
\end{pmatrix}.
$$
can be computed by 

```{r}
cor(cbind(jumpreach, hairlength, gender01)) |> 
round(digits = 2)
```

You should, however, never trust single number. Always also **look at the data**! 

```{r}
pairs(cbind(jumpreach, hairlength, gender01), 
      lower.panel = NULL)
```

#### **Conditional Distribution** {-}

The conditional density of $Y$ given $X_1 = x_1, \ldots, X_K = x_K$ is

$$
f_{Y \mid X_1, \ldots, X_K}(y \mid x_1, \ldots, x_K)
= \frac{f_{Y, X_1, \ldots, X_K}(y, x_1, \ldots, x_K)}
       {f_{X_1, \ldots, X_K}(x_1, \ldots, x_K)},
$$
where $f_{X_1, \ldots, X_K}$ is the joint density of $X_1,\dots,X_K$. 



## Conditional Mean: The Regression Function {#sec-regression-function}

The **conditional mean** of $Y_i$ given $X_{i} = x$ is the expected jump and reach score for all students with observed hair length equal to $x$:
$$
E(Y \mid X = x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\, f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x)\, dy.
$$

When we view 
$$
m(x) = E(Y_i \mid X_i = x)
$$ 
as a function of $x$, it is called the **regression function**. It describes how the average outcome changes across different observed values of the predictor.

There are generally two types of estimation approaches: 

* Making a parametric functional form assumption for the regression function $m(x) = E(Y \mid X = x),$ and then estimate the parameters (@sec-linearRegCh)
* Making no parametric functional form assumptin and estimate $m(x) = E(Y \mid X = x)$ directly from the data, for instance, by estimating the conditional mean $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\, f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x)\, dy$ for different values of $x.$

#### 🤸‍♀️ **Jump-Test Data Example** {-}

When we do not want to make a parametric functional form assumption for the regression function $E(Y \mid X = x),$ we can take our three estimates of the conditional densities $f_{Y \mid X}(y \mid x)$ shown in @fig-conditional-hists: 

* Group 1 **low jump reach scores:** 
    * `grp1` contains jump reach scores in $[0,10]$ cm. 
    * Average hair length in this group is $x=$ `group_mid[1]` $=$ `r round(group_mid[1], 2)`

* Group 2 **middle jump reach scores:**     
    * `grp2` contains intermediate jump reach scores in $(10,25]$ cm. 
    * Average hair length in this group is $x=$ `group_mid[2]` $=$ `r round(group_mid[2], 2)`

* Group 3 **high jump reach scores:**     
    * `grp3` contains high jump reach scores $>25$ cm. 
    * Average hair length in this group is $x=$ `group_mid[3]` $=$ `r round(group_mid[3], 2)`

That is, we can estimate the following three conditional means: 

* $E(Y|X= `r round(group_mid[1], 2)` )  \approx \widehat{E}(Y|X= `r round(group_mid[1], 2)` )=$ `mean(grp1)` $=$ `r round(mean(grp1), 2)` 
* $E(Y|X= `r round(group_mid[2], 2)` )  \approx \widehat{E}(Y|X= `r round(group_mid[2], 2)` )=$ `mean(grp2)` $=$ `r round(mean(grp2), 2)` 
* $E(Y|X= `r round(group_mid[3], 2)` )  \approx \widehat{E}(Y|X= `r round(group_mid[3], 2)` )=$ `mean(grp3)` $=$ `r round(mean(grp3), 2)` 


```{r}
#| label: fig-cond-mean
#| fig-cap: "Scatter plot with estimated conditional means for the three hair-length groups (red points) and the true (usually unknonw) regression function $E(Y\\mid X=x)$ (dashed black line). The estimated means track the true line closely."

cond_mean <- c(mean(grp1), mean(grp2), mean(grp3))

plot(jumptest_data$hairlength, jumptest_data$jumpreach,
     xlab = "Hair Length (cm)", ylab = "Jump Height (cm)",
     pch  = 19, col = "grey70", axes = FALSE)
axis(1, at = c(round(group_mid[1],2), 10, round(group_mid[2],2), 25, round(group_mid[3],2)))     
axis(2)
box()
abline(a = beta1, b = beta2, col = "black", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
abline(v = c(10,25))
mtext(c("Group 1", "Group 2", "Group 3"), side = 3, line = 0.5,
      at = c(group_mid[1], group_mid[2], group_mid[3]))
points(group_mid, cond_mean,
       pch = 19,
       col = "tomato",
       cex = 1.8)
legend("topright",
       legend = c(expression(hat(E)(Y ~ "|" ~ X == x)),
                  expression(E(Y ~ "|" ~ X == x))),
       col    = c("tomato", "black"),
       pch    = c(19, NA),
       lty    = c(NA, 2),
       lwd    = c(2, 2),
       bty    = "n")
```

The three estimated conditional means fall roughly on a line. This visual observation motivates parametrizing the regression function as straight line function:
$$
E(Y_i \mid X_i = x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x.
$$

Provided that this functional form assumption is correct, we only have to estimate the unknown parameters $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ to get a good estimate of $E(Y_i \mid X_i = x).$

The next chapter (@sec-linearRegCh) develops the method for estimating $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ from the observed pairs 
$$
(Y_{1,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{1,\operatorname{obs}}),\, (Y_{2,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{2,\operatorname{obs}}),\, \ldots,\, (Y_{n,\operatorname{obs}},\, X_{n,\operatorname{obs}})
$$ 
and for assessing how precisely these estimates pin down the true line.


## Important Distributions {#sec-distributions}

Regression analysis relies on three distributions for statistical inference. They are introduced here as a reference; their roles in hypothesis testing and model evaluation are developed in @sec-linearRegCh.

### The Normal Distribution {#sec-normal}

The most important distribution in statistics is the **normal distribution** (also called the Gaussian distribution). A random variable $X$ follows a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$, written $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, if its density is

$$
f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}
       \exp\!\left\{-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right\},
\quad x \in \mathbb{R}.
$$

The normal density is **symmetric** and **bell-shaped**, with:

- $\mu$ controlling the **location** (center) of the distribution, and
- $\sigma > 0$ controlling the **spread** (how wide the bell is).

The special case $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$ is called the **standard normal distribution**, denoted $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.

```{r}
#| label: fig-normal-densities
#| fig-cap: "Normal distributions with different means and standard deviations."

curve(dnorm(x, mean = 0, sd = 1),
      xlim = c(-7, 9), ylim = c(0, 0.65),
      ylab = "Density", xlab = "x",
      col  = "steelblue", lwd = 2)
curve(dnorm(x, mean = 2, sd = 1),
      add = TRUE, col = "tomato", lwd = 2)
curve(dnorm(x, mean = 0, sd = 2),
      add = TRUE, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = c(expression(mu == 0 ~ "," ~ sigma == 1),
                  expression(mu == 2 ~ "," ~ sigma == 1),
                  expression(mu == 0 ~ "," ~ sigma == 2)),
       col    = c("steelblue", "tomato", "darkgreen"),
       lwd    = 2, bty = "n")
```

R provides four functions for any distribution: `d` (density), `p` (cdf), `q` (quantile), `r` (random draws). For the normal:

```{r}
set.seed(42)
rnorm(n = 5, mean = 0, sd = 1)       ## 5 random draws from N(0,1)
dnorm(x = 0, mean = 0, sd = 1)       ## density at x = 0
pnorm(q = 1.96, mean = 0, sd = 1)    ## P(Z <= 1.96)
qnorm(p = 0.975, mean = 0, sd = 1)   ## z such that P(Z <= z) = 0.975
```

The normal distribution occupies a central place in statistics for two reasons. First, many natural measurements (heights, exam scores, measurement errors) are approximately normally distributed. Second, the **Central Limit Theorem** guarantees that the average of a large number of independent random variables is approximately normally distributed, regardless of the original distribution. This result underpins the inference methods we develop in the next chapter.

A particularly useful fact for standardizing: if $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1).
$$

#### Estimating the Normal Parameters from Data {-}

Assuming normality means we assume $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The distribution is then fully characterized by two **unknown parameters**, $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$, which we estimate from the observed values $X_{1,\operatorname{obs}}, \ldots, X_{n,\operatorname{obs}}$:

$$
\hat{\mu}_{\operatorname{obs}} = \bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_{i,\operatorname{obs}},
\qquad
\hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{obs}} = s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n
  (X_{i,\operatorname{obs}} - \bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}})^2.
$$

In R, `mean()` computes $\hat{\mu}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ and `var()` computes $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{obs}}.$

Substituting $\hat{\mu}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ and $\hat{\sigma}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ into the normal density formula gives the **fitted normal density estimator**

$$
\hat{f}_{X,\operatorname{obs}}^{\operatorname{norm}}(x)
= \frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}_{\operatorname{obs}}\sqrt{2\pi}}
  \exp\!\left\{-\frac{(x - \hat{\mu}_{\operatorname{obs}})^2}{2\hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{obs}}}\right\}.
$$

This is a **parametric** approach: it assumes a specific functional form (the bell curve) and only estimates the two free parameters. The non-parametric estimators $\hat{f}_{X,\operatorname{obs}}^{\operatorname{hist}}$ and $\hat{f}_{X,\operatorname{obs}}^{\operatorname{kernel}}$ from @sec-pdf make no such functional form assumption. The trade-off is flexibility versus efficiency: parametric estimates use fewer degrees of freedom and can be more precise — but only if the assumed distribution is correct.

@fig-hairlength-normal overlays $\hat{f}_{X,\operatorname{obs}}^{\operatorname{norm}}$ on the histogram of hair lengths. The bell curve does **not** fit well: the two peaks (one for short-haired, one for long-haired students) are a feature a single normal distribution cannot capture. This is a reminder that distributional assumptions should always be checked against the data.

```{r}
#| label: fig-hairlength-normal
#| fig-cap: "Histogram of hair lengths with the fitted normal density $\\hat{f}_{X,\\operatorname{obs}}^{\\operatorname{norm}}$ (dashed red) and the true mixture density $f_X(x)$ (solid black). The normal fit misses both peaks because the true distribution is a 50/50 mixture of two normal components — a reminder that distributional assumptions should always be checked."

mu_hat  <- mean(jumptest_data$hairlength)
sig_hat <- sd(jumptest_data$hairlength)

hist(jumptest_data$hairlength,
     freq     = FALSE,
     col      = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.4),
     border   = "white",
     xlab     = "Hair Length (cm)",
     main     = "")
curve(f_X(x), add = TRUE, col = "black", lwd = 2)
curve(dnorm(x, mean = mu_hat, sd = sig_hat),
      add = TRUE, col = "tomato", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = c(expression(hat(f)["X,obs"]^"hist" * "(x)"),
                  expression(hat(f)["X,obs"]^"norm" * "(x)"),
                  expression(f[X](x))),
       pch    = c(15, NA, NA),
       lty    = c(NA, 2, 1),
       lwd    = c(NA, 2, 2),
       col    = c(adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.4), "tomato", "black"),
       bty    = "n")
```

### The Chi-Squared Distribution {#sec-chisqdist}

The **chi-squared distribution** with $\nu$ degrees of freedom, written $W \sim \chi^2_\nu$, is defined as the distribution of the sum of $\nu$ squared independent standard normal random variables:

$$
W = Z_1^2 + Z_2^2 + \cdots + Z_\nu^2 \sim \chi^2_\nu,
\quad Z_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0,1).
$$

Key properties: the distribution is **right-skewed** with support on $[0, \infty)$. Its mean is $\nu$ and its variance is $2\nu$. As $\nu$ grows, the distribution becomes more symmetric and shifts rightward.

```{r}
#| label: fig-chisq-densities
#| fig-cap: "Chi-squared densities for various degrees of freedom. The distribution shifts right and becomes more symmetric as $\\nu$ increases."

curve(dchisq(x, df = 1), xlim = c(0, 20), ylim = c(0, 0.5),
      ylab = "Density", xlab = "x", col = 1, lwd = 2)
for (df_val in c(2, 3, 5, 10)) {
  curve(dchisq(x, df = df_val), add = TRUE, col = df_val, lwd = 2)
}
legend("topright",
       legend = paste("ν =", c(1, 2, 3, 5, 10)),
       col    = c(1, 2, 3, 5, 10),
       lwd    = 2, bty = "n")
```

The chi-squared distribution arises naturally in regression analysis when testing hypotheses about model coefficients. We will encounter it in @sec-multipleLR-inference.


### The F-Distribution {#sec-fdistrib}

The **F-distribution** with numerator degrees of freedom $m$ and denominator degrees of freedom $n$, written $Y \sim F_{m,n}$, is the distribution of the ratio of two independent chi-squared random variables each divided by their degrees of freedom:

$$
Y = \frac{W_1/m}{W_2/n} \sim F_{m,n},
\quad W_1 \sim \chi^2_m,\quad W_2 \sim \chi^2_n,\quad W_1 \perp W_2.
$$

Like the chi-squared, the F-distribution has support on $[0, \infty)$ and is right-skewed. Its shape depends on both $m$ and $n$.

```{r}
#| label: fig-f-densities
#| fig-cap: "F-distribution densities for selected degrees of freedom."

curve(df(x, df1 = 1, df2 = 20), xlim = c(0, 6), ylim = c(0, 1.3),
      ylab = "Density", xlab = "x",
      col  = "steelblue", lwd = 2)
curve(df(x, df1 = 3, df2 = 20),
      add = TRUE, col = "tomato", lwd = 2)
curve(df(x, df1 = 5, df2 = 20),
      add = TRUE, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = c("F(1, 20)", "F(3, 20)", "F(5, 20)"),
       col    = c("steelblue", "tomato", "darkgreen"),
       lwd    = 2, bty = "n")
```

The F-distribution is used in regression for the **F-test**, which asks whether a set of predictors jointly explains variation in the outcome. We return to this in @sec-multipleLR-inference.

## The Data-Generating Process {#sec-dgp}

We now have all the building blocks to formalize the concept introduced in @sec-where-data.

::: {.callout-note}
## Data-Generating Process

A **data-generating process (DGP)** is the complete probability model assumed to have produced the observed data. It specifies:

1. Which random variables are involved and what their distributions are.
2. How different variables are related — in particular, the conditional distribution of the outcome $Y$ given the predictor(s) $X$.
3. How the observations are drawn (usually: as independent draws from the same distribution).
:::

For a so-called **simple linear regression** (staight line), the DGP is written as

$$
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i,
\quad
\varepsilon_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0,\, \sigma^2),
\quad i = 1, \ldots, n.
$$
where 

* $Y_i$ is called the outcome or dependent variable
* $X_i$ is called the predictor or regressor variable
* $\varepsilon_i$ is a statistical "error" term that captures everything of $Y_i$ that is not explained by $ \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i.$ Typically, we assume (hopefully it's true) that $E(\varepsilon_i|X_i)=0.$

Moreover, often we assume an **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) sampling:** That is, we assume: 

1. Each random pairs
$$
(Y_i, X_i)\quad\text{and}\quad(Y_j,X_j)\quad\with $i\neq j$
$$
are independent of each other; i.e., the realization of $(Y_i, X_i)$ is not affected by the realization of $(Y_j, X_j)$ for any $j\neq i.$  
2. All random pairs 
$$
(Y_i, X_i)\quad i=1,2,\dots,n
$$
have the same distribution. 




::: {.callout-important}

## The Usually Unknown Part of the DGP

Usually, we do not know the underlying distributions of $(Y_i, X_i),$ but let us say we know them to be as following: 

* $X_i\sim\mathcal{U}[1,45]$ for all $i=1,2,\dots$
* $\beta_1=77$ and $\beta_2=-1.75$
* $\varepsilon_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ with $\sigma = 5$

such that the conditional distribution of $Y_i$ given $X_i$ is

$$
Y_i |X_i \sim \mathcal{N}( \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i , \sigma^2) 
$$

:::

In parametric regression analysis, we aim for estimating the unknown parameters 

* $\beta_1$, 
* $\beta_2$, and 
* $\sigma^2$ 

based on one observed realization 
$$
(Y_{1,\text{obs}}, X_{1,\text{obs}}), \ldots, (Y_{n,\text{obs}}, X_{1,\text{obs}})
$$
of the so-called **(i.i.d) random sample**
$$
(Y_1, X_1), \ldots, (Y_n, X_n).
$$



### Simulating from a DGP {-}

Because the DGP specifies a complete probability model, we can **simulate** from it: we draw artificial data according to the model, with known parameter values. Simulation is a powerful pedagogical tool — it lets us see what data the DGP would produce if the parameters were known, and check whether our estimation methods recover those parameters.

The code below simulates jump and reach and hair-length data from a linear DGP with known $\beta_0$, $\beta_1$, and $\sigma$.

```{r}
#| label: fig-dgp-simulation
#| fig-cap: "Left: data simulated from a known DGP (red line = true regression function). Right: actual class survey data. The simulated data and the real data have a similar qualitative appearance."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

set.seed(42)
n_sim <- 200

## True DGP parameters
beta1 <- 77     ## expected jump & reach when hair length = 0 (extrapolation)
beta2 <- -1.75  ## each extra cm of hair reduces expected jump & reach by 1.75
sigma <-  7     ## noise level (standard deviation of errors)

## Simulate predictor values
x_sim <- runif(n_sim, min = 1, max = 50)

## Simulate outcomes from the DGP
eps_sim <- rnorm(n_sim, mean = 0, sd = sigma)
y_sim   <- beta1 + beta2 * x_sim + eps_sim
y_sim   <- pmax(y_sim, 0)  ## scores cannot be negative

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

## Simulated data
x_line <- c(1, 50)
y_line <- beta1 + beta2 * x_line

plot(x_sim, y_sim,
     xlab = "Hair Length (cm)", ylab = "Jump Height (cm)",
     main = "Simulated from DGP",
     pch  = 19, col = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.4))
lines(x_line, y_line, col = "tomato", lwd = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = expression(E(Y ~ "|" ~ X == x) == beta[0] + beta[1] * x),
       col = "tomato", lwd = 2, bty = "n")

## Actual class data
plot(jumptest_data$hairlength, jumptest_data$jumpreach,
     xlab = "Hair Length (cm)", ylab = "Jump Height (cm)",
     main = "Class Survey Data",
     pch  = 19, col = adjustcolor("grey40", alpha.f = 0.7))

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

The red line in the left panel is the **true regression function** $E(Y \mid X = x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$. In the real problem (right panel), this line is unknown — we have to estimate it from data. Chapter @sec-linearRegCh shows how to do that using OLS.

Notice that each point in the data falls at a vertical distance from the true line: the distance is the error $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. Some students score higher on the jump and reach test than the regression line predicts ($\varepsilon_i > 0$); others score lower ($\varepsilon_i < 0$). The distribution of these deviations is normal with mean zero, which is the key assumption of the linear regression DGP.


### Random Samples and i.i.d. {#sec-iid}

The DGP above assumes that each observation is drawn **independently** and from the **same** distribution. This is the **i.i.d. assumption** (independent and identically distributed).

::: {.callout-note}
## Random Sample

A collection of observations $(X_1, Y_1), (X_2, Y_2), \ldots, (X_n, Y_n)$ is a **random sample** if:

1. **Independent:** Each pair $(X_i, Y_i)$ is independent of all other pairs $(X_j, Y_j)$ for $j \neq i$.
2. **Identically distributed:** Every pair $(X_i, Y_i)$ is drawn from the same joint distribution $F_{X,Y}$.
:::

In the class survey, the i.i.d. assumption is reasonable if: students filled in the questionnaire independently of each other (no copying, no groups), and each student can be regarded as a random draw from the same population (all business administration students at this university, say).

The i.i.d. assumption is the default in cross-sectional studies, where we observe a large number of units (people, firms, countries) at a single point in time. It underpins the statistical properties of the OLS estimator that we derive in the next chapter.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Association Is Not Causation

The slope $\beta_1 \approx -1.75$ in our DGP says that students who differ by 1 cm in hair length differ, on average, by $-1.75$ cm in jump and reach. But this is a **statistical association**, not a **causal** claim. Cutting your hair will not improve your jump and reach score.

The association in the data is almost entirely driven by an unobserved variable — **gender** — that affects both hair length and physical fitness. Statisticians call this **confounding**: a third variable that influences both $X$ and $Y$, creating a spurious correlation between them.

This distinction — association versus causation — is one of the central themes of the next chapter. After introducing OLS and showing how to estimate and interpret $\hat\beta_1$, @sec-whatdoesbeta asks what $\hat\beta_1$ really tells us. The short answer is: it estimates a conditional association, which may or may not reflect a causal effect.
:::


## Summary


::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The Law of Large Numbers guarantees that $\bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ and $\bar{Y}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ converge to $E(X)$ and $E(Y)$  as $n \to \infty$. 

In other words, for largish sample sizes, we can consider the values of $\bar{X}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ and $\bar{Y}_{\operatorname{obs}}$ as good estimates of the usually unknown $E(X)$ and $E(Y).$ 

However, they are always only estimates (think like guessing machines) of the true (usually unknown) counterparts. 
:::


## References {-}
